subsections.title: Java 9-11 Features
subsections.keyRule: Java 9: Module system (JPMS), JShell, Collection factory methods. Java 10: var. Java 11: String API improvements, HttpClient stable, Files.readString/writeString. Each version adds useful incremental improvements.

terms.what: JPMS (Java Platform Module System): declare modules with module-info.java. Modules explicitly declare what they export and what they require. Strong encapsulation of JDK internals.
terms.why: Before Java 9, the classpath was one big ball — any code could access any class. Modules enforce explicit dependency boundaries and hide internal APIs.
terms.how: module com.myapp.service { requires com.myapp.db; exports com.myapp.service.api; } — only .api package visible outside. com.myapp.db must also be a module.
terms.where: Large modular applications, framework development, microkernel architectures.
terms.when: Use JPMS for large complex applications needing strict encapsulation. Most Spring Boot apps use classpath — modules are optional. Many libraries still not fully modularized.
terms.issue: Many legacy libraries not yet module-compatible — automatic modules work but lose strong encapsulation. Complex to set up in large codebases.
terms.fix: Start with classpath (Spring Boot default). Add JPMS when you need strict package visibility or are building a library. Automatic module name in MANIFEST.MF for partial adoption.

star.a: Java 9 module system — explicit exports/requires in module-info.java. Strong encapsulation. JDK itself is modularized. Replaced monolithic rt.jar.

terms.what: JShell: interactive Java REPL — try code without creating a class/project. Collection.of(): List.of(), Set.of(), Map.of() — create immutable collections in one line (Java 9+).
terms.why: JShell for quick experiments. List.of() replaces Arrays.asList() and Collections.singletonList() — shorter, truly immutable.
terms.how: JShell: $ jshell → jshell> var list = List.of(1,2,3); list.get(0); → 1. Code: List<String> names = List.of('Alice','Bob','Carol'); — immutable, no nulls.
terms.where: JShell: learning, prototyping, quick tests. List.of(): anywhere you create small immutable collections.
terms.when: Use List.of() instead of new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList(...)) for immutable collections. JShell for quick API exploration.
terms.issue: List.of() is truly immutable — list.add() throws UnsupportedOperationException. Also: no null elements allowed.
terms.fix: For mutable collections: new ArrayList<>(List.of(a,b,c)). For collections that might have nulls: Arrays.asList() or new ArrayList<>().

star.s: Interview: what changed in Java 9 collections?
star.a: List.of(), Set.of(), Map.of() — immutable collection factory methods. Shorter than Arrays.asList(), truly immutable (no add/set/remove). Null elements forbidden.

terms.what: Java 9 added Stream methods: takeWhile(predicate) — take elements until predicate false. dropWhile(predicate) — drop elements until predicate false. Stream.iterate(seed, predicate, nextFunc) — bounded iterate without limit().
terms.why: takeWhile/dropWhile are more expressive for ordered streams — take sorted elements up to a condition.
terms.where: Ordered stream processing, bounded sequence generation, while-condition stream operations.

terms.what: Java 11 added String methods: isBlank() (true for empty or whitespace), strip()/stripLeading()/stripTrailing() (Unicode-aware trim), lines() (stream of lines), repeat(n) (repeat string n times).
terms.why: Old trim() doesn't handle Unicode whitespace. isBlank() cleaner than isEmpty() || s.trim().isEmpty(). lines() replaces split('\n').
terms.where: String validation, text processing, CSV parsing, template processing.
terms.when: Use isBlank() instead of isEmpty() when whitespace-only strings should be treated as empty. Use strip() instead of trim() for Unicode safety.
terms.issue: trim() only strips ASCII whitespace (chars <= 32). strip() handles all Unicode whitespace. Using trim() on user input from international apps may fail.
terms.fix: Replace trim() with strip(). Replace isEmpty() with isBlank() for whitespace checks.

star.s: International user's name with Unicode space not being cleaned.

terms.what: Java 11 made java.net.http.HttpClient stable. Supports HTTP/2, async requests, streaming. Files.readString(path) and Files.writeString(path, content) — read/write whole files in one line.
terms.why: Old HttpURLConnection was verbose. New HttpClient is modern, type-safe, async. Files.readString() replaces boilerplate FileReader + BufferedReader.
terms.how: HttpClient client = HttpClient.newHttpClient(); HttpRequest req = HttpRequest.newBuilder(URI.create(url)).build(); HttpResponse<String> resp = client.send(req, BodyHandlers.ofString()); Files.readString(Path.of('file.txt'));
terms.where: HTTP client calls, file I/O, test helpers.
terms.when: Use HttpClient for all new HTTP calls — replaces Apache HttpClient, RestTemplate for simple cases. Files.readString() for reading small-to-medium files.
terms.issue: HttpClient send() is blocking. Use sendAsync() for non-blocking: CompletableFuture<HttpResponse<String>> future = client.sendAsync(req, BodyHandlers.ofString());
terms.fix: Use sendAsync() for production code. Use send() in tests where blocking is acceptable.

star.s: Apache HttpClient dependency for simple HTTP calls.
star.r: No third-party dependency. Same functionality. Cleaner code.

terms.what: Java 14+ NullPointerExceptions include a detailed message saying WHICH variable was null: 'Cannot invoke String.length() because user.getName() is null' instead of just 'NullPointerException'.
terms.why: Old NPE messages just said 'NullPointerException at line X' — you had to guess which variable was null. New messages pinpoint it immediately.

terms.what: Switch expressions (preview Java 12, stable Java 14) return a value, use arrow labels (→), and require exhaustive cases. Java 17 finalizes switch expressions with sealed classes for total coverage.
terms.why: Old switch was verbose, fall-through bug-prone, and couldn't return values. New switch is expression-oriented and safer.
terms.how: String result = switch(day) { case MONDAY, TUESDAY -> 'Weekday'; case SATURDAY, SUNDAY -> 'Weekend'; default -> 'Other'; }; // No fall-through. Arrow syntax. Returns value.
terms.where: Status/enum/type-based logic, replacing long if-else chains, pattern matching in switch (Java 21).
terms.when: Use switch expression whenever switch is the right construct. It's cleaner and safer than the old switch statement.
terms.issue: Old switch fall-through: forgetting break causes unintended fall-through to next case. Switch expression arrows don't fall through.
terms.fix: Migrate old switch statements to switch expressions. Arrow syntax enforces no fall- through.

star.s: Old switch statement with missing break causing wrong status calculation.
star.a: Converted to switch expression with arrow syntax. No fall-through possible.

terms.what: Sealed classes restrict which classes can extend or implement them. The permitted subclasses form a closed, known hierarchy — the compiler knows all possibilities.
terms.why: For modeling closed domains: Shape can only be Circle, Square, or Triangle. Exhaustive pattern matching possible — compiler ensures all cases handled.
terms.how: sealed interface Shape permits Circle, Square, Triangle {} final class Circle implements Shape { double radius; } — only Circle, Square, Triangle can implement Shape.
terms.where: Domain modeling (payment types, event types, result types), replacing enum when each type needs different fields, pattern matching.
terms.when: Use sealed when you need a closed type hierarchy — all subtypes known at compile time. Alternative to enum when types need different data.
terms.issue: Adding a new permitted type requires changing the sealed class — not open for extension by default.
terms.fix: This is intentional — sealed classes are closed hierarchies. For open extension, use regular interface/class.

star.s: Interview: when would you use sealed class vs enum?
star.a: Sealed when each type has different fields/behavior (Circle with radius vs Square with side). Enum when types are just labels with same structure. Sealed enables exhaustive pattern matching.

terms.what: Record Patterns let you deconstruct records in switch/instanceof patterns: case Person(String name, int age) when age > 18 → adult handling.
SequencedCollection/SequencedMap/SequencedSet interfaces add: getFirst(), getLast(), reversed() to List, Set, Map. Unified API for ordered collections.
terms.why: Before Java 21: List had get(0)/get(size-1). Deque had peekFirst/peekLast. LinkedHashSet had no first/last methods. Sequenced Collections unify this.
terms.how: List.of(1,2,3).getFirst() → 1. List.of(1,2,3).getLast() → 3. list.reversed() → reversed view. LinkedHashSet now has getFirst()/getLast().
terms.where: All ordered collections in Java 21+. Replace get(0) with getFirst() for clarity.
terms.when: Use in Java 21+ code. getFirst()/getLast() throw NoSuchElementException on empty collections — handle accordingly.
terms.issue: getFirst()/getLast() throw NoSuchElementException if collection empty — unlike Optional-returning alternatives.
terms.fix: Check isEmpty() before calling getFirst()/getLast(), or use try-catch.

star.s: Interview: what's new in Java 21 collections?
star.a: SequencedCollection — getFirst(), getLast(), reversed() added to List, Deque, LinkedHashSet, LinkedHashMap. Unified ordered collection API.

terms.what: Structured Concurrency (incubating): StructuredTaskScope — parent thread waits for all subtasks. Failure propagates clearly. Cancels sibling tasks when one fails.
terms.why: Traditional CompletableFuture: if one subtask fails, others continue until explicitly cancelled. StructuredTaskScope automatically handles parent-child task lifetime.
terms.how: try (var scope = new StructuredTaskScope.ShutdownOnFailure()) { var user = scope.fork(() -> fetchUser(id)); var order = scope.fork(() -> fetchOrder(id)); scope.join().throwIfFailed(); process(user.resultNow(), order.resultNow()); }
terms.where: Microservice fan-out calls, parallel data fetching, request-scoped parallel operations.
terms.when: Java 21+ with virtual threads. Use when you need clear parent-child task relationships and clean failure handling.
terms.issue: Still incubating in Java 21 — API may change. Not recommended for stable production code yet.
terms.fix: Use CompletableFuture.allOf() for stable production. Experiment with StructuredTaskScope for Java 21+ internal tools.

star.s: Fetch user + order + preferences in parallel — if any fails, cancel others.

terms.what: ScopedValue provides immutable, thread-local-like values for virtual threads. Unlike ThreadLocal, ScopedValue is immutable and has bounded scope — value only set within a specific code scope.
terms.why: ThreadLocal is mutable and stateful — problematic with virtual threads (many threads, thread reuse). ScopedValue is immutable and scoped — safe and predictable.

terms.what: LocalDate: date only (2024-01-15). LocalTime: time only (14:30:00.123). LocalDateTime: date + time, NO timezone. All are immutable.
terms.why: Old java.util.Date/Calendar: mutable, confusing, not thread-safe. java.time: immutable, clear, thread-safe.
terms.how: LocalDate date = LocalDate.of(2024, 1, 15); LocalDate today = LocalDate.now(); LocalDateTime dt = LocalDateTime.of(date, LocalTime.of(14, 30));
terms.where: Business dates, form inputs, log timestamps (no timezone), database dates (DATE, DATETIME columns).
terms.when: LocalDate for date-only (birthday, appointment date). LocalDateTime for DB timestamps without timezone. ZonedDateTime when timezone matters.
terms.issue: LocalDateTime stored in DB without timezone — when server timezone changes, saved times are misinterpreted.
terms.fix: For app events visible to users in multiple timezones: use ZonedDateTime or Instant + timezone stored separately.

star.s: Appointment system shows wrong times after server moved to different timezone.
star.a: Changed from LocalDateTime to ZonedDateTime for appointment storage.
star.r: Times display correctly in all timezones.

terms.what: ZonedDateTime: date + time + timezone — for user-facing times across zones. Instant: machine timestamp (epoch milliseconds) — for ordering events, logs. ZoneId: timezone identifier like 'Asia/Kolkata'.
terms.why: Instant is absolute — same moment everywhere. ZonedDateTime converts to local time for display. Don't confuse: Instant for storage, ZonedDateTime for display.
terms.how: Instant now = Instant.now(); ZonedDateTime kolkata = now.atZone(ZoneId.of('Asia/Kolkata')); Instant back = kolkata.toInstant(); // same moment.
terms.where: Instant: DB timestamps, event ordering, duration calculation. ZonedDateTime: user-visible scheduled times, calendar apps.
terms.when: Store timestamps as Instant or epoch millis. Convert to ZonedDateTime in user's timezone for display.
terms.issue: Storing ZonedDateTime with timezone in DB — timezone changes in DB cause incorrect times.
terms.fix: Store as Instant (epoch millis) in DB. Read timezone from user profile. Convert on display: instant.atZone(userZone).

star.s: International app shows meetings at wrong time for users in different timezones.
star.a: Store meeting time as Instant. Show in user's ZoneId.
star.r: All users see correct local time.

terms.what: Duration: time-based amount (hours, minutes, seconds, nanos). Period: date-based amount (years, months, days). DateTimeFormatter: parse and format date/time objects.
terms.why: Duration for measuring elapsed time and time gaps. Period for calendar-based differences (3 months, 2 years). Formatter for string conversion.
terms.how: Duration.between(start, end).toMinutes(). Period.between(birthDate, today).getYears() → age. DateTimeFormatter fmt = DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern('dd/MM/yyyy'); fmt.format(date); LocalDate.parse('15/01/2024', fmt);
terms.where: Age calculation, session duration, interval scheduling, date formatting for display and parsing from forms.
terms.when: Duration for time-of-day/second-level differences. Period for date-level differences. Formatter for all parsing/formatting.
terms.issue: DateTimeFormatter is thread-safe (unlike SimpleDateFormat). But custom patterns with wrong letters cause DateTimeParseException.
terms.fix: Test formatters with example dates. Use standard ISO formatters (DateTimeFormatter.ISO_DATE) when possible.

star.r: Clean, correct age calculation. Handles leap years automatically.
